features.popup:
38PN129 — Hagood Creek Petroglyph Site

Cherokee Lower Towns territory · SC's only protected rock art

Location: Hagood Mill Historic Site, Pickens County (34.927°N, 82.722°W)

South Carolina's most significant rock art site and the only publicly accessible, protected petroglyph assemblage in the state. At least 32 carvings — including 18 human figures, predominantly male — are pecked into a large, low-lying sandstone boulder adjacent to Hagood Creek, a tributary of the Twelve Mile River. The site lies in the heart of the Cherokee Lower Towns, along the foothills of the Blue Ridge Mountains.

Discovered January 2003 by Michael Bramlett, a volunteer with the SC Petroglyph Survey, on a rainy day. Much of the boulder had been buried beneath the 1820s Pickens-to-Rosman road, which paradoxically preserved the carvings. Excavation revealed 6 additional human figures and 2 abstract petroglyphs beneath the roadbed — proving they predate the 1820s. Four buried figures are male, enclosed in individual squares with cupola/dome enclosures on top. One head has horn-like appendages. The striking 'Refrigerator Man' has a head and legs but no arms, with a rectangular filled torso. Historic carvings (initials and 'Thomas') were found on exposed portions only — none beneath the road.

Part of a broader pattern across the SC Piedmont. Tommy Charles's survey (1997–ongoing) documented 64 rock art sites and over 1,000 individual carvings across the Upstate. The circle-and-line motif found repeatedly mirrors patterns along the Appalachian Mountains from Alabama to New York. A purpose-built museum structure now protects the petroglyphs in situ with colored lighting.

Visitor info: Hagood Mill Historic Site, 138 Hagood Mill Rd, Pickens — free admission, Wed–Sat 10am–4pm.

Sources: Charles 2004, 2010 (Discovering SC's Rock Art, USC Press); Bramlett 2003; SC Encyclopedia; Diaz-Granados & Duncan 2004, Ch. 15; SCIAA

38PN81 — Pickens County Lowland Site

Cherokee territory · Richest lowland petroglyph site in SC — 198 carvings

Location: Pickens County, near stream at north end of small floodplain (approximate)

Richest lowland petroglyph site in South Carolina with 198 total petroglyphs — the greatest number recorded at any lowland site. Located near a stream where a creek cascades from higher ground (a historic gristmill was later built at this location). The first prehistoric rock art site discovered during the 1997 survey, found on a rainy day.

All prehistoric glyphs are extremely eroded and totally invisible during the day — discovered using lights at night. Motifs include 4 anthropomorph-like figures (3 in a single panel, 1 alone), 2 serpent-like forms, and 169+ abstract petroglyphs. Named glyphs include 'the keyhole,' 'the lag,' and 'the horseshoes.' Talc powder tracing revealed the full extent of the carvings. One of three 'special' lowland sites at the north end of small floodplains near low falls.

Sources: Charles 2004, 2010; SCIAA

38OC378 — Oconee County Lowland Site

Cherokee territory · One of SC's most significant rock art sites

Location: Oconee County, opposite shore of stream from hillside outcrop (approximate)

One of South Carolina's most abundant and significant rock art sites. Discovered by sheer luck — a rock protruding from a hillside with a small vertical groove running contrary to the natural horizontal orientation. After excavation using steel probes, 15 large boulders were located and uncovered. Four boulders had abstract petroglyphs and cupules considered prehistoric.

Two anthropomorph-like figures boldly pecked into the top of a large gneiss ledge — the most boldly carved anthropomorphs recorded by the survey, visible by day regardless of light conditions. Associated cupules, a 'bird track' glyph, and abstract motifs. Cupules are incorporated into petroglyphs by connecting grooves. Rock surfaces so decomposed that a thin veneer could be removed by pinching. One of three sites where additional petroglyphs were discovered by excavating buried portions. Landowner offered the site to a heritage preservation group.

Sources: Charles 2004, 2010; SCIAA

38LU422 — Laurens County Multi-Motif Site

Catawba territory · Rare combination of anthropomorphic, zoomorphic & abstract carvings

Location: Laurens County, near a recently constructed lake (approximate)

Multi-motif lowland petroglyph site with a rare combination of anthropomorphic, zoomorphic, and abstract carvings. Two prehistoric human figures in stick form are highly eroded and totally invisible during the day until just before sunset, when low-angle light skims the rock surface.

One carving suggestive of a quadruped and possibly another zoomorph — associated with the two human figures and several abstract motifs. Abstract petroglyphs partially inundated by a recently constructed lake — most glyphs extremely eroded, observable only at night with lights or at sunset. Other carvings are said to exist below the water. The site also has clearly visible historic petroglyphs (names, initials, dates). Trinomial from original 1997 survey article.

Sources: Charles 1997, 2004, 2010; SCIAA

38SP13 — Spartanburg Steatite Quarry

Catawba territory · Late Archaic steatite quarry with birdlike figure · NRHP-listed

Location: Spartanburg County, immediately by a river (approximate, NRHP-listed)

A single human figure with birdlike attributes on the vertical wall of a steatite boulder at a huge prehistoric steatite quarry dating to the Late Archaic period (~4,000 years ago). Many stone bowls and fragments found in various stages of completion — evidence of large-scale soapstone bowl manufacturing. The figure has a well-defined body and limbs with scratches creating a birdlike feathering effect — possibly a composite carving (body incised by one person, feathering added later). Located immediately by a river, just above ground level. One of the few lowland glyphs visible by daylight without enhancement. When first reported, covered in green moss. Listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

Sources: Charles 2004, 2010; NRHP; SCIAA

38RD668 — 'King Beetle Rock'

Catawba territory · Zoomorphic petroglyph in Columbia

Location: Columbia, Richland County — thickly settled old neighborhood (approximate)

A large granite boulder in a thickly settled old neighborhood in the heart of Columbia with a beetle-like image boldly pecked into its vertical face. Locals call it 'King Beetle Rock' and older residents recall playing around it as children. The only petroglyph recorded in Richland County and one of the two southernmost petroglyphs in South Carolina. Presumed prehistoric but dating uncertain. Anomalously visible by day — the granite host rock (rather than gneiss, which hosts most SC rock art) may weather less rapidly, preserving the carving's visibility.

Sources: Charles 2004, 2010; SCIAA

38OC184 — Tugaloo River Turtle

Cherokee territory · Incised turtle motif — endangered by vehicle traffic

Location: Oconee County, immediately next to Tugaloo River (approximate)

Single turtle motif created by incising rather than pecking — unusual technique for SC rock art. Located in a precarious position immediately next to the Tugaloo River on one side and a narrow dirt road on the other. Endangered by increasing vehicle traffic — the turtle's head has been partially abraded by vehicle tires. Recorded in 1983, initially thought to be the first example of rock art in SC (actually the second, after 38GR9 in 1979). Located in the last parcel of Cherokee land ceded to South Carolina on March 22, 1816. No direct cultural affiliation with the Cherokee can be confirmed.

Sources: Charles 2004, 2010; SCIAA

38LX273 — Lexington County Anthropomorph

Catawba territory · Southernmost SC petroglyph — narrowly escaped destruction

Location: Lexington County (approximate)

A single anthropomorphic figure — the only rock art recorded in Lexington County. Two cupules are incorporated into the carving, one representing the 'head' and another at the pelvis. Visible during the day but difficult to photograph due to tree shadow. This site narrowly escaped destruction when a new roadway was cut through the forest for a housing development, missing the boulder by approximately ten feet. One of only two petroglyphs recorded this far south in South Carolina (the other being 'King Beetle Rock' in Columbia). Granite host rock, like 38RD668, may account for daylight visibility.

Sources: Charles 2004, 2010; SCIAA

38GR9 — First SC Petroglyph

Cherokee territory · First petroglyph ever recorded in South Carolina (1979)

Location: Greenville County (approximate)

The first petroglyph ever recorded in South Carolina. Filed as a brief report in 1979 by Dr. Robert L. Stephenson, then state archaeologist and director of SCIAA. The report consists of a single photograph and site-location data. Local people called the carved stone the 'Old Indian Rock,' believing it was created by Native Americans, though some thought the carving might be Spanish in origin. This discovery, along with two others reported in 1983, was the catalyst for launching the systematic South Carolina Rock Art Survey in 1997.

Sources: Charles 2004, 2010; Stephenson 1979; SCIAA

38GR303 — Spring Site

Cherokee territory · Faintly incised abstract glyphs near collapsed spring alcove

Location: Greenville County (approximate)

Discovered by chance while visiting an elderly woman's old spring site. The stone alcove over the spring had collapsed and the area was now mature forest. About 25 meters from the spring, a small boulder (156 × 55 cm) was covered with unusual faintly incised abstract petroglyphs — created by incising instead of the more common pecking technique. Accompanying the glyphs are 23 shallow holes (~5–7 mm width) that appear to have been drilled, possibly using a hollow cane, a known prehistoric drilling method.

Sources: Charles 2004, 2010; SCIAA

38GR320 — Church Cemetery Wall

Cherokee territory · 4 petroglyphs incorporated into 1786 church cemetery wall

Location: Greenville County (approximate)

Four petroglyphs incorporated into a stone wall surrounding a church cemetery dating to 1786 (earliest marked burial 1791). Around 1932, a new portion was added using locally collected stone brought by wagon. Among the rocks were four carved stones. The builders recognized them and placed them side-by-side adjacent to the entrance gate. Three were created by pecking; the fourth possibly pecked then smoothed by rubbing. They are believed to be prehistoric — the stones were collected from the surrounding countryside, not carved in place.

Sources: Charles 2004, 2010; SCIAA

38FA305 — Fairfield County Cupule Site

Catawba territory · Only prolific cupule site in SC — 200+ cupules

Location: Fairfield County (approximate)

The only site prolific with cupules recorded in the South Carolina Rock Art Survey. More than 200 cupules were found randomly placed on several flat, adjacent sheet rocks near ground level. One larger depression, like a mortar, is among them. Nearby elevated rocks also have cupules on tops and vertical faces. Whether these are prehistoric or historic cannot be definitively determined, but they are not consistent with modern quarrying patterns (which produce larger, uniform, in-line holes). The cupules are randomly distributed, small but variable in size, shallow, and have worn, irregular edges.

Sources: Charles 2004, 2010; SCIAA

38PN122 — Highland Circle Site

Cherokee territory · Hundreds of circles on sloping rock dome

Location: Pickens County highlands (approximate)

Hundreds of circles — and no other forms — on a sloping rock dome in the Pickens County highlands. The first site where the SC Rock Art Survey discovered petroglyphs bold enough to be visible by daylight. Circles are less numerous approaching the timberline at the top of the dome and decrease near the bottom as well. One of several similar circle sites in a relatively restricted area of Pickens County. Why so many redundant circles occur on these particular rock domes, and not on similar ones nearby, has not been determined.

Sources: Charles 2004, 2010; SCIAA

38PN121 — Highland Spine Site

Cherokee territory · Marked motif variance on narrow rock spine

Location: Pickens County highlands (approximate)

Located on a sharp, narrow rock spine leading to the slightly higher 'circle' sites, this highland petroglyph site is the smallest in the area but the only one exhibiting marked motif variance. Enclosed on both sides by low forest, it lacks the expansive westward vistas typical of most high-elevation sites. Most circles here have outward-radiating grooves or are dissected by interior grooves. Some are joined by grooves to other motifs, forming complexes. Several standalone petroglyphs consist of irregular squares, rectangles, and randomly meandering lines.

Sources: Charles 2004, 2010; SCIAA

38PN127 — Highland East-Facing Site

Cherokee territory · Only east-facing rock dome with petroglyphs in SC

Location: Pickens County highlands (approximate)

Only two petroglyphs — one a simple, faintly visible circle, the other a slightly more visible circle with six outward-radiating lines (unusual because the lines are paired in groups of two). The only circles found on an east-facing rock dome — all other highland circle sites are west-facing. Their limited number, east-facing location, and paired radiating lines set this site apart from all other high-elevation petroglyph sites.

Sources: Charles 2004, 2010; SCIAA

38GR305 — Stone Barn Wall Petroglyph

🪨 Portable (displaced) · Greenville County · Prehistoric carving incorporated into barn wall

Location: Greenville County (approximate)

A carved stone that was incorporated into a permanent stone barn wall, giving it formal site status (trinomial 38GR305). The carving was removed from an unknown original location. Along with the four stones at 38GR320 (church cemetery wall), this is one of only two portable petroglyphs given formal SCIAA trinomials — because both were incorporated into permanent structures. All other portable glyphs were assigned identity numbers instead. The stone was likely collected locally, suggesting the original carving site was nearby in the Greenville County Piedmont.

Sources: Charles 2010 (Fig. 95); SCIAA

38GR306

Greenville County · Petroglyph Site

Rock art site recorded during the South Carolina Rock Art Survey (Charles 1997–2010). Prehistoric origin. Approximate location — county-level placement only; exact coordinates restricted by SCIAA to protect sites on private land.

Sources: Charles 2004, 2010; SCIAA

38GR307 — Greenville County Humus Site

Cherokee territory · Petroglyphs revealed beneath forest humus

Location: Greenville County (approximate — exact location restricted by SCIAA)

Two petroglyphs discovered after removing the accumulated humus layer from a rock outcrop. Part of the broader pattern across the SC Piedmont where centuries of topsoil erosion — accelerated by mid-nineteenth century land clearing and agriculture — buried archaeological sites at the bases of hills and on floodplains. The site demonstrates why systematic rock cleaning is essential to the survey methodology: many SC petroglyph sites are invisible until vegetation and soil are carefully removed.

Sources: Charles 2010 (Plate 6b, 7); SCIAA

38PN138 — Mountaintop Oddity

Cherokee territory · Possible zoomorph on highland site

Location: Pickens County mountaintop (approximate)

A petroglyph on one of the mountaintop sites in Pickens County, discovered and photographed by volunteer Jeff Catlin while hiking. The carving could be interpreted as a turtle — or it could be a fortuitous combination of circles and lines that just happens to resemble one. Unlike most highland petroglyphs in the region, which are exclusively circles, this site has a zoomorphic-like form. Visible by day without enhancement, consistent with the highland pattern where glyphs are boldly carved into exposed rock domes.

Sources: Charles 2010 (Fig. 28); SCIAA

38OC384 — Talc Powder Revelation

Cherokee territory · Alphabet-like petroglyphs revealed by talc powder technique

Location: Oconee County (approximate — exact location restricted by SCIAA)

A rock where volunteer Michael Bramlett spotted a groove in daylight while eating lunch. When the survey team covered the rock with black plastic to simulate night conditions and used lights to skim the surface, they could detect the presence of carving but were unable to define or photograph it adequately. Bramlett then spread dry talc powder over the rock surface; after brushing away the excess, alphabet-like petroglyphs were revealed that spelled nothing recognizable. This site demonstrates one of the survey's innovative field techniques for documenting extremely eroded carvings invisible under normal conditions.

Sources: Charles 2010 (Plate 5); SCIAA

38PN102 — Highland Pictograph

Cherokee territory · Pictograph in rock shelter — red/orange ochre

Highland pictograph in a rock shelter, painted in red/orange ochre. Located in the Table Rock / Oolenoy River area of Pickens County. Possibly the site James Mooney reported in 1891 during his ethnographic work with the Eastern Cherokee. One of only three pictograph sites recorded in South Carolina — the vast majority of SC rock art consists of petroglyphs (carved) rather than pictographs (painted).

Sources: Charles 2004, 2010; Mooney 1891; SCIAA

38PN134 — Lowland Pictograph

Cherokee territory · Lowland pictograph site

Lowland pictograph site in Pickens County. One of only three pictograph sites recorded in South Carolina. Approximate location — county-level placement only; exact coordinates restricted by SCIAA.

Sources: Charles 2004, 2010; SCIAA

38KE281 — Southernmost SC Pictograph

Catawba territory · Southernmost pictograph in South Carolina — red/orange ochre

Lowland pictograph site in Kershaw County, painted in red/orange ochre. The southernmost pictograph recorded in South Carolina. One of only three pictograph sites in the state. Approximate location — county-level placement only; exact coordinates restricted by SCIAA.

Sources: Charles 2004, 2010; SCIAA

Cherokee Lower Towns Territory

Keowee River drainage · Oconee, Pickens, Anderson Counties

The Cherokee Lower Towns occupied the Keowee River drainage in the Piedmont foothills of northwestern South Carolina. Principal towns included Keowee, Seneca, Toxaway, Jocassee, Estatoe, and Tomassee. The Lower Towns served as the primary interface between the Cherokee Nation and English colonial traders from Charleston. Destroyed by Colonel Andrew Williamson's expedition in 1776; many former town sites now submerged beneath Lake Keowee and Lake Jocassee.

Sources: SC Encyclopedia; Bartram 1775–76; Wikipedia (Historic Cherokee settlements)

Catawba Nation Territory

Iswa — People of the River · Catawba River Valley

The Catawba Nation — the only federally recognized tribe in South Carolina — inhabited the Catawba River Valley spanning the Piedmont of both Carolinas. At contact, their territory encompassed most of the SC Piedmont from the Blue Ridge foothills to the fall line. The Nation was a confederacy incorporating 20+ smaller tribes who sought protection from Iroquois raids and colonial warfare. Renowned for their warrior culture and the oldest continuously practiced pottery tradition east of the Mississippi. The 1763 reservation of 144,000 acres was reduced to 630 acres by 1850. Federal recognition restored 1993.

Sources: Catawba Indian Nation; Merrell 1989; SC Encyclopedia; UNC Catawba Project

Portable Petroglyph #1 — Glassy Mountain

🪨 Portable · Greenville County · Found during road grading

Find spot: Glassy Mountain, Greenville County (approximate)

Found on Glassy Mountain during grading for a road into a new development — the first portable petroglyph reported to the survey after the team advertised in newspapers for information about rock carvings. When found, only a portion of the glyph was visible beneath a thin veneer of limonite, which the owner removed to expose the full carving. The carvings consist of a small circle dissected by interior lines (similar to the circle-and-line motif found across the Appalachians) and several abstract lines. Stone measures 31 × 16 cm. Created by incising.

Sources: Charles 2010 (Fig. 53); SCIAA

Portable Petroglyph #2 — Greenville Woods

🪨 Portable · Northern Greenville County · Now at Pickens County Museum

Find spot: Northern Greenville County, in woods (approximate)

Found by a young girl playing in the woods of northern Greenville County. One end is marred by what appears to be a plow scar. The glyph end is stained red, apparently with ochre. The carvings consist of unidentifiable abstract motifs created by incising. The stone measures 88 × 18 cm, 10 cm thick. Donated to the Pickens County Museum where it is on display.

Sources: Charles 2010 (Fig. 54); SCIAA

Portable Petroglyph #3 — Anderson County

🪨 Portable · Anderson County · Three triangular motifs

Find spot: Anderson County (approximate)

A stone bearing three somewhat triangular-shaped motifs, found in Anderson County. The survey recorded several petroglyphs inscribed with similar lines creating simple squares and triangles. Stone measures 40 × 19 cm, 12 cm thick. Created by incising. Among the simplest of the portable petroglyph designs but representative of the geometric repertoire found across the northwestern Piedmont.

Sources: Charles 2010 (Fig. 55); SCIAA

Portable Petroglyph #4 — Spartanburg Farm

🪨 Portable · SW Spartanburg County · Well-incised markings

Find spot: Southwestern Spartanburg County farm (approximate)

Found on a farm in southwestern Spartanburg County, this stone was removed from the field where it was found and is now stored in an old abandoned tenant home on the property. The glyphs consist of a series of well-incised but unidentifiable markings. Stone measures 71 × 29 cm, approximately 15 cm thick. Created by incising.

Sources: Charles 2010 (Fig. 56); SCIAA

Portable Petroglyph #5 — Lake Jocassee Stone

🪨 Portable · Pickens County · Elaborately engraved, possibly Mississippian

Find spot: Shore of Lake Jocassee, Pickens County — eroded from banks during lake construction

One of several elaborately engraved stones found on Lake Jocassee when the lake was being filled after construction. Eroded from the banks by rising water. This small engraved stone has glyphs on both sides. One side has a small circle in the center with four panels radiating outward, each containing an irregularly shaped groove somewhat similar to a lightning bolt, with unidentifiable motifs near the perimeter. The other side features a serpent, a shield-like figure, and a half circle. Stone measures 10.4 cm across. Possibly Mississippian in origin (~1000–1500 CE). The find spot — Lake Jocassee — submerged the Cherokee Lower Towns of Jocassee and Toxaway.

Sources: Charles 2010 (Fig. 57); SCIAA

Portable Petroglyph #6 — Oconee Mountains

🪨 Portable · Oconee County · Smallest carved stone reported

Find spot: Mountains of Oconee County, during dirt road repair (approximate)

A tabular stone found in the mountains of Oconee County when a dirt road was being repaired. The stone appears to have been naturally shaped and polished in a stream prior to being inscribed. It is one of the smallest carved stones reported in the survey. The incised lines are quite small and the motif is an unidentifiable abstract form. Stone measures 26 × 18 cm, approximately 7 cm thick. Includes drilled holes alongside the incised motifs.

Sources: Charles 2010 (Fig. 59); SCIAA

Portable Petroglyph #7 — Lake Jocassee Masterwork

🪨 Portable · Pickens County · Most elaborately decorated stone in the survey · Possibly Mississippian

Find spot: Shore of Lake Jocassee, Pickens County — found by a family walking along the shore in the late 1960s

The most elaborately decorated stone recorded by the entire SC Rock Art Survey. Found by a family walking along the shore of Lake Jocassee during the late 1960s, when wave action was eroding the landform. Unlike most small portables, this glyph is deeply incised and has small, shallow holes drilled into its surface — apparently by cane, as the bottoms of several have the raised centers typical of hollow-cane drilling. Motifs include a sun-like circle with radiating lines, what appears to be a half moon, and several unidentifiable markings. A portion of the carving is missing where the stone broke in antiquity — the broken edge is weathered equally with the rest of the rock. Possibly Mississippian (~1000–1500 CE). Like Portable #5, found on the shores of Lake Jocassee, which submerged the Cherokee Lower Towns.

Sources: Charles 2010 (Fig. 59); SCIAA

Portable Petroglyph #8 — Charleston Birdman Gorget

🪨 Portable · Charleston County · Mississippian birdman motif · Only rock carving south of the fall line

Find spot: Bank of a tidal creek, Charleston County

A small gorget (6.3 × 4.3 cm) — the only example of a rock carving reported from south of the fall line in South Carolina, making it a significant outlier from the Piedmont concentration. Found eroding from the bank of a tidal creek in Charleston County, broken into two pieces. Associated with several chipped-stone tool preforms and a cluster of burned-limestone chunks that may be the remains of a prehistoric hearth. The carving represents a stylized bird similar to the birdman motif in Mississippian-period iconography (~1000–1500 CE), connecting it to the Southeastern Ceremonial Complex. Two suspension holes at the top indicate it was worn as a pendant.

Sources: Charles 2010 (Fig. 60); Birmingham & Eisenberg 2000; Brown 2004, 2007; Strong 1989; SCIAA
